[Page 3] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 3 1. OS Fundamentals & Architecture 1.1 What is an Operating System? An Operating System (OS) is system software that manages computer hardware and software resources and provides common services for computer programs. It acts as an intermediary between the user and the hardware. Without an OS, every programmer would need to directly program the hardware — an impractical and error-prone task. Resource Manager Allocates CPU, memory, I/O devices among competing processes fairly and efficiently. Hardware Abstraction Hides hardware complexity — programs use simple calls like open(), read(), write(). Security & Protection Prevents unauthorised access to memory, files, and devices. Process Coordinator Creates, schedules, and terminates processes; manages multitasking. n Real-Life Example The OS is like a hotel manager. Guests (programs) don't directly enter the kitchen (hardware). The manager (OS) takes orders, allocates rooms, manages resources, and ensures guests don't interfere with each other. 1.2 Types of Operating Systems Type Description & Examples Batch OS Jobs collected in batches, processed sequentially with no user interaction. CPU never idle. Example: early IBM systems, payroll processing. Drawback: no interactivity. Multiprogramming Multiple jobs in memory simultaneously. When one waits for I/O, CPU switches to another. Increases CPU utilisation. Example: Running Word + Chrome together. Multitasking Extension of multiprogramming with time-sharing. Each user gets a small time slice. Appears simultaneous. Example: Linux, Windows desktop. Real-Time (RTOS) Strict time deadlines — missing a deadline = system failure. Hard RTOS (must meet deadline) vs Soft RTOS (best effort). Example: pacemaker, ABS brakes, air traffic control. Distributed OS Multiple independent computers networked to appear as one. Resources shared transparently. Example: Google data centres, Hadoop clusters. Clustered OS Tightly coupled computers sharing storage and working together. High availability — if one fails, another takes over. Example: Oracle RAC.

[Page 4] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 4 1.3 OS Architecture — Layered Model & Kernel Types The OS is structured in layers, each providing services to the layer above it. The kernel is the core — it runs in privileged mode and has direct hardware access. User programs run in user mode and access hardware only through system calls. OS Architecture — Layered Model & Kernel Types User Applications Shell / System Calls API File System & I/O Mgmt Process & Memory Mgmt Device Drivers Hardware (CPU/RAM/Disk) Monolithic vs Microkernel Monolithic Process Mgmt Memory Mgmt File System Device Drivers IPC Network Hardware All in kernel space Microkernel User Space File System Device Drivers IPC Network Kernel Sched+IPC+Mem Hardware Minimal kernel space Monolithic Kernel All OS services (process mgmt, memory, file system, device drivers, IPC, networking) run in a single large kernel in kernel space. Very fast — direct function calls. Drawback: a bug in any module can crash the entire OS. Example: Linux, Unix, MS-DOS. Microkernel Only essential services (scheduling, basic IPC, memory management) in kernel. Everything else (file system, drivers) runs as user-space servers. More secure and stable — a crashed driver doesn't crash OS. Drawback: message passing between user/kernel space adds overhead (slower). Example: Mach, QNX, Minix. Hybrid Kernel Combines both: keep performance-critical parts in kernel like monolithic, but run some services in user space like microkernel. Best of both worlds. Example: Windows NT, macOS (XNU kernel). Layered OS OS divided into N layers. Layer 0 = hardware, Layer N = user interface. Each layer uses services of the layer below it. Easy to debug (test layer by layer). Drawback: each layer call adds overhead. n UPPSC Favourite — Microkernel vs Monolithic Q: 'Why is Microkernel more secure but slower than Monolithic?' A: In Microkernel, services like file system and device drivers run in user space. If a driver crashes, only that service fails — the OS continues (MORE SECURE). But every service call requires a message to pass from user space to kernel space and back — this context switching overhead makes it SLOWER than Monolithic where everything is a direct function call in the same address space.

[Page 5] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 5 1.4 System Calls A system call is a programmatic way for a user-space program to request a service from the OS kernel. When a program makes a system call, it switches from user mode to kernel mode (mode switch / trap), the kernel performs the requested service, then switches back. Process fork() — create child process, exec() — run new program, exit() — terminate, wait() — wait for child File open() — open file, read() — read data, write() — write data, close() — close file, stat() — file info Device ioctl() — device control, read()/write() — device I/O, mmap() — memory-mapped I/O Information getpid() — get process ID, time() — current time, getuid() — user ID, uname() — system info Communication pipe() — create pipe, socket() — network socket, send()/recv() — send/receive data, signal() — send signal n Example — System Call in C When you write: open('data.txt', O_RDONLY) Your C program makes a system call. CPU switches to kernel mode. The kernel checks permissions, locates the file, and returns a file descriptor. CPU switches back to user mode. Without system calls, user programs could directly access hardware — catastrophically dangerous!

[Page 6] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 6 2. Process Management 2.1 What is a Process? A process is a program in execution. A program is a passive entity (a file on disk); a process is an active entity with its own memory, CPU registers, and state. A thread is a lightweight process — multiple threads share the same process memory but have their own program counter, stack, and registers. n Process Control Block (PCB) The OS maintains a PCB (also called Task Control Block) for each process containing: • Process ID (PID) • Process State • Program Counter • CPU Registers • Memory Management info (page tables) • I/O Status • Accounting info (CPU time used) The PCB is created at process creation and deleted when the process terminates. 2.2 Process States & Transitions Every process moves through these five states during its lifetime. The OS scheduler decides which transitions happen and when. Process State Transition Diagram New Ready Running Waiting Terminat admit dispatch I/O request exit Preempted (time-quantum expired) I/O complete → back to Ready New → Ready Process created (fork()). OS initialises PCB. Process enters ready queue. Ready → Running CPU scheduler picks this process (dispatch). Only one process runs per CPU core at a time. Running → Waiting Process needs I/O (disk read, network, keyboard input) — voluntarily releases CPU. Waiting → Ready I/O operation completes. Process re-enters ready queue to wait for CPU again. Running → Ready Time quantum expires (preemption) or higher-priority process arrives.
[Page 7] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 7 Running → Terminated Process calls exit() or is killed. OS reclaims all resources. PCB deleted.

[Page 8] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 8 2.3 CPU Scheduling Algorithms The CPU scheduler selects a process from the ready queue and allocates the CPU to it. Good scheduling maximises CPU utilisation and throughput while minimising waiting time, turnaround time, and response time. Expect 3–5 numerical questions in UPPSC! CPU Scheduling — Gantt Charts: FCFS vs SJF vs Round Robin FCFS P1 P2 P3 P4 P5 0 6 10 18 21 26 SJF P4 P2 P5 P1 P3 0 3 7 12 18 26 RR(q=4) P1 P2 P3 P4 P5 P1 P3 P5 0 4 8 12 15 19 21 25 26 P1 BT=6 P2 BT=4 P3 BT=8 P4 BT=3 P5 BT=5 FCFS Non-preemptive. Simple queue — first in, first served. Problem: Convoy Effect — a long CPU-bound process can block many short ones, causing high average waiting time. SJF Non-preemptive. Run shortest burst time first. Provably optimal for minimum average waiting time. Problem: Starvation — long processes may never run if short processes keep arriving. Also needs burst time in advance. SRTF Preemptive SJF. If a new process arrives with burst time less than remaining time of current process, preempt it. Best average waiting time. Same starvation problem. Round Robin Preemptive. Each process gets a fixed time quantum (q). After quantum expires, process goes to end of ready queue. Time-sharing standard. If q very large → FCFS; q very small → too many context switches. Priority Each process has a priority number. Lower number = higher priority (or vice versa — varies). Preemptive or non-preemptive. Problem: Starvation (low-priority processes never run). Solution: Aging — gradually increase priority of waiting processes. Multilevel Queue Ready queue divided into separate queues: system processes, interactive, background. Each queue has its own algorithm. Processes don't move between queues. n Solved Numerical — FCFS

[Page 9] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 9 Processes: P1(AT=0,BT=5), P2(AT=1,BT=3), P3(AT=2,BT=8), P4(AT=3,BT=2) (AT=Arrival, BT=Burst) Gantt: | P1 (0-5) | P2 (5-8) | P3 (8-16) | P4 (16-18) | Completion: P1=5, P2=8, P3=16, P4=18 Turnaround (CT-AT): P1=5, P2=7, P3=14, P4=15 Avg TAT = (5+7+14+15)/4 = 10.25 ms Waiting (TAT-BT): P1=0, P2=4, P3=6, P4=13 Avg WT = (0+4+6+13)/4 = 5.75 ms n Solved Numerical — Round Robin (q=2) Processes: P1(BT=5), P2(BT=3), P3(BT=4) all arrive at t=0 Gantt: |P1(0-2)|P2(2-4)|P3(4-6)|P1(6-8)|P2(8-9)|P3(9-11)|P1(11-12)| Completion: P1=12, P2=9, P3=11 Waiting: P1=12-5=7, P2=9-3=6, P3=11-4=7 Avg Waiting = (7+6+7)/3 = 6.67 ms 2.4 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Cooperating processes need to exchange data and synchronise their actions. Two fundamental IPC models exist, each with its own trade-offs. IPC — Shared Memory vs Message Passing Shared Memory (Faster) Process A Process B Shared Memory Buffer [0][1][2]...[n] write read No kernel involvement each r/w → Fast but needs synchronisation Message Passing (Safer) Process A Process B OS Kernel Message Queue send() / recv() Every msg goes through kernel → Safer, no sync needed, slower Shared Memory Processes establish a region of shared memory. Producer writes data, consumer reads. The OS is involved only to set up the shared region — not for each data exchange. Extremely fast. Requires explicit synchronisation (semaphores/mutexes) to prevent race conditions. Message Passing Processes communicate via send() and receive() system calls. Every message goes through the OS kernel. Safer — no synchronisation needed. Easier to implement in distributed systems. Slower because every exchange involves a kernel trap. Example: Unix pipes, sockets, message queues.

[Page 10] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 10 3. Process Synchronisation & Deadlocks 3.1 The Critical Section Problem A critical section is a segment of code that accesses shared resources (shared variables, files, etc.). If two processes execute their critical sections simultaneously, a race condition occurs — the output depends on the order of execution, leading to data corruption. Any correct solution must satisfy three conditions: Mutual Exclusion Only ONE process can be in its critical section at any given time. No two processes run critical sections concurrently. Progress If no process is in its critical section and some processes want to enter, only those not in their remainder section can participate in deciding who enters next — and this decision cannot be postponed indefinitely. Bounded Waiting After a process requests to enter its critical section, there is a bound on how many times other processes are allowed to enter before it. No starvation. Peterson's Solution Software solution for 2 processes. Uses: flag[i]=true (want to enter), turn=j (give turn to other). Satisfies all 3 conditions but requires busy waiting. n Race Condition Example Two bank threads both read balance=500, both add 100, both write 600. Final balance = 600 (WRONG — should be 700!) The critical section here is: read balance → add → write balance. Must be executed atomically. 3.2 Semaphores A semaphore is an integer variable accessed only through two atomic operations: wait() and signal(). Introduced by Dijkstra. They solve the critical section problem and can coordinate processes.

[Page 11] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 11 Semaphore — wait(S) and signal(S) Operation Flow wait(S) — Acquire / Lock S = S − 1 S < 0 ? Yes Block Process Add to wait queue Process suspended — waits No Enter Critical Section Resource acquired! signal(S) — Release / Unlock S = S + 1 S ≤ 0 ? Yes Wake a Process Remove from wait queue Blocked process resumes No return S>0 : resource free | S=0 : no resource, none waiting | S<0 : |S| processes in wait queue Binary Semaphore Value is 0 or 1. Exactly like a mutex (mutual exclusion lock). Initial value = 1. One process locks, others wait. Example: protecting a critical section. Counting Semaphore Value ranges from 0 to N. Used to control access to a resource pool of N instances. Initial value = N (number of resources). Example: 3 printers available → semaphore initialised to 3. Mutex vs Semaphore Mutex: ownership — only the thread that locked it can unlock it. Semaphore: any thread can signal. Semaphores more flexible but harder to use correctly. Busy Wait vs Block Spinlock (busy-wait): process loops checking condition — wastes CPU. Blocking semaphore: process added to wait queue, CPU given to others — more efficient. 3.3 Classic Synchronisation Problems Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Producer generates data → puts in buffer. Consumer takes from buffer. Problems to solve: (1) Producer must wait if buffer FULL. (2) Consumer must wait if buffer EMPTY. (3) Only one process accesses buffer at a time. Solution: 3 semaphores — mutex=1 (mutual exclusion), empty=N (free slots), full=0 (filled slots). Readers-Writers Multiple readers can read simultaneously (no data corruption). But a writer needs exclusive access. Problem 1: readers priority — writers may starve. Problem 2: writers priority — readers may starve. Solution uses semaphores with a read-count variable. Dining Philosophers 5 philosophers around a table, 5 forks. Each needs 2 forks to eat. If all pick up left fork simultaneously → deadlock. Solutions: (1) Allow only 4 philosophers to sit simultaneously. (2) Asymmetric: odd picks left-then-right, even picks right-then-left. (3) Monitor-based solution.

[Page 12] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 12 3.4 Deadlocks A deadlock is a situation where a set of processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by another process in the set. Like a traffic gridlock — no one can move. Deadlock — Resource Allocation Graph & 4 Necessary Conditions Circular Wait → DEADLOCK R1 R2 P1 P2 P3 P4 Each Pi waits for resource held by Pi+1 All 4 Must Hold Simultaneously 1. Mutual Exclusion Resource held by only one process at a time. AND 2. Hold & Wait Holds resource, waiting for another. AND 3. No Preemption Resources can't be forcibly taken away. AND 4. Circular Wait P1→P2→P3→...→P1 wait cycle exists. ⇒ DEADLOCK OCCURS 3.5 Deadlock Handling Strategies Prevention Ensure at least one of the 4 conditions never holds. Examples: (1) No Hold & Wait — request all resources at once (poor resource utilisation). (2) Allow Preemption — if process can't get next resource, release all current ones. (3) No Circular Wait — impose total ordering on resource types; request only in increasing order. Avoidance Allow the system to dynamically examine resource-allocation state to ensure circular-wait never occurs. Requires processes to declare MAXIMUM needs in advance. Banker's Algorithm checks if granting a request keeps the system in a SAFE STATE. Detection & Recovery Allow deadlock to occur. Periodically run detection algorithm (Resource Allocation Graph or Wait-For Graph). If deadlock found: (a) Abort one or more processes. (b) Preempt resources (rollback processes to checkpoints). (c) Consider victim selection — abort process with least work done. Ignore (Ostrich Policy) Pretend deadlocks never occur. Used when deadlocks are very infrequent and the cost of prevention/detection outweighs the occasional restart. Used by many commercial OS including early Unix and Windows (rare deadlocks handled by user reboot).

[Page 13] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 13 Banker's Algorithm — Safe State Detection Flow Start: New Request Need ≤ Available? No Deny Request Process waits Yes Pretend to Allocate Update Available, Allocation System in Safe State? Yes Grant Request Actual allocation No Rollback Restore old state, wait Safe Sequence A safe sequence is an ordering of all processes such that each process can get resources it needs from currently available + resources held by processes before it in the sequence. Example: Available:[3,3,2] Safe sequence: P1→P3→P4→P0→P2 3

[Page 14] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 14 4. Memory Management 4.1 Address Types & Basic Concepts Logical / Virtual Address Generated by CPU during execution. What the programmer/process sees. Range: 0 to max (defines logical address space). Translated to physical by MMU at runtime. Physical Address Actual location in RAM hardware. What memory chips respond to. Range depends on installed RAM. Different from logical address (except in simple systems). MMU Memory Management Unit — hardware component that translates logical → physical addresses at runtime using page tables or segment tables. Swapping Temporarily move an entire process from RAM to disk (backing store) to free memory for other processes. Bring it back when needed. Very slow (disk I/O). Contiguous Allocation Process stored in one continuous block of memory. Simple but causes fragmentation. External fragmentation: free space scattered in small chunks. Internal fragmentation: allocated block larger than needed. Fragmentation External: enough total free space but not contiguous. Fix: compaction (shuffle memory — expensive). Internal: allocated frame bigger than page — small waste inside each frame. 4.2 Paging & Translation Paging solves external fragmentation by dividing both physical memory and logical address space into equal fixed-size blocks. Physical blocks = frames. Logical blocks = pages. A page table maps page numbers to frame numbers. Pages of a process need not be contiguous in RAM. Memory Management — Paging with TLB Address Translation CPU GeneratesnLogical Addr Page # Offset p d Logical Address TLB Cache Recent page↔framenmappings TLB Lookup HIT MISS Page Table (in RAM — slower) Frame # Offset f d Physical Address RAM PhysicalnM Physical Address = Frame Number × Page Size + Offset EAT = α×t_TLB + (1−α)×(t_TLB + t_mem) where α = TLB hit ratio

[Page 15] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 15 TLB Hit Page number found in TLB. Physical address computed in 1 memory cycle. EAT ≈ TLB access time (very fast, ~1–10 ns). TLB Miss Page not in TLB. Must access page table in RAM (1 extra memory access). TLB updated with new entry (LRU replacement). EAT increases. Multi-level Page Table Single-level page table may be too large (e.g., 32-bit address with 4KB pages = 1M entries × 4 bytes = 4MB per process!). Solution: two-level or three-level page tables — only load the parts needed. Inverted Page Table One entry per physical frame (not per virtual page). Saves space. But searching is slow — need to search by (PID, page) pair. Used with hashing. Segmentation Divide process into logical segments: code, data, stack, heap. Variable size. Each segment has a base and limit register. Can have external fragmentation. Combined with paging → paged segmentation. 4.3 Virtual Memory & Page Replacement Virtual memory allows processes to use more address space than physically available RAM. Only the pages currently needed are kept in RAM (demand paging). When a process accesses a page not in RAM, a page fault occurs — the OS loads the page from disk. Virtual Memory — Demand Paging & Thrashing Demand Paging Flow CPU Access Page In RAM? No Page Fault! Trap to OS Load Page from Disk Resume Process Yes → Continue Thrashing Concept Number of Processes CPU Utilisation THRASHINGnZONE Peak

[Page 16] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 16 LRU Page Replacement — Full Trace (Frames = 3, Reference String: 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,4,2) 7 7 F 0 7 0 F 1 7 0 1 F 2 2 0 1 F 0 2 0 1 H 3 2 0 3 F 0 2 0 3 H 4 4 0 3 F 2 4 2 3 F 3 4 2 3 H 4 4 2 3 H 2 4 2 3 H Reference String → F=Fault H=Hit Total Page Faults = 7 | Hits = 5 | Fault Rate = 7/12 = 58.33% FIFO Replace oldest page in memory. Simple. Suffers Belady's Anomaly: adding more frames can paradoxically INCREASE page faults! Not used in practice. Optimal (OPT) Replace page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. Guaranteed minimum page faults. Impractical — can't predict future. Used only as a theoretical benchmark. LRU Least Recently Used. Replace page that hasn't been used for the longest past time. Good approximation of Optimal. Practical. Implemented using counters or stack. Does NOT suffer Belady's Anomaly. LFU Least Frequently Used. Replace page with fewest access counts. Problem: a page used heavily early but no longer needed stays in memory too long. Working Set Track the set of pages used in the last ∆ time units. Keep all working set pages in memory. If working set of all processes > available frames → suspend some processes to prevent thrashing. nn Thrashing Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time paging (swapping pages in/out) than executing useful work. Cause: Too many processes competing for too little physical memory — each page fault evicts a page another process immediately needs. Symptom: CPU utilisation drops sharply even though many processes are 'running'. Fix: Working Set Model — only run processes whose entire working set fits in memory. Suspend others.

[Page 17] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 17 5. Storage & File Systems 5.1 File Management A file is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage. The OS provides a uniform logical view of storage through the file system, hiding hardware details. File System — Tree-Structured Directory & File Access Methods / (root)	/ (root) /bin /etc /home /var user1 user2 docs pics file.txt Tree-Structured Directory File Access Methods	Sequential Read from start, one record at a time. Cursor moves forward only. Fast for streaming. Direct/Random Jump to any record using block number. Fast for databases. Indexed Index table maps keys to record positions. Best for large files with searches. File Attributes Name, Type (extension), Size, Location (disk address), Protection (permissions), Timestamps (created, modified, accessed), Owner. Sequential Access Records read/written in order, from beginning to end. Simple and efficient for streaming. Example: reading a log file line by line. Direct / Random Access Any record accessed directly by specifying its position (block number). Essential for databases where records must be found quickly. Indexed Access An index file stores pairs of (key, pointer-to-record). Look up key in index, follow pointer. Good for large files with frequent key searches. Single-Level Directory All files in one flat directory. Simple but name conflicts arise between users. Example: very old systems. Two-Level Directory Separate directory for each user. Resolves name conflicts. Each user has their own directory. Cannot share files easily. Tree-Structured Hierarchical. Users can create subdirectories. Files identified by absolute path (/home/user/docs/file.txt) or relative path. Standard in all modern OS. 5.2 Disk Scheduling Algorithms Disk access time = seek time + rotational latency + transfer time. Seek time dominates — disk scheduling minimises head movement. UPPSC regularly asks 3–5 numerical questions here.

[Page 18] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 18 Disk Scheduling — SCAN vs C-SCAN Head Movement (Head starts at 53) 0 50 100 150 200 14 37 6567 98 122	124 183	53 SCAN: 299 cyl SCAN 0 50 100 150 200 14 37 6567 98 122	124 183	53 C-SCAN: 322 cyl C-SCAN FCFS Service requests in arrival order. Simple, fair. No starvation. Poor performance — head may move back and forth repeatedly across the disk. Good only when load is light. SSTF Shortest Seek Time First. Go to nearest pending request. Better throughput than FCFS. Problem: Starvation — requests at far end of disk may wait indefinitely as near requests keep arriving. Similar to SJF scheduling. SCAN (Elevator) Move head in one direction servicing all requests until end of disk, then reverse and service requests in the other direction. Like a building elevator. Fair — no starvation. Better than SSTF. C-SCAN Circular SCAN. After reaching the end, jump back to the beginning (no servicing on return trip) and sweep again. More uniform wait times than SCAN because requests near the start don't wait for the full return sweep. LOOK Like SCAN but head only goes as far as the last request in each direction (not to the physical end of disk). More efficient than SCAN — less unnecessary travel. C-LOOK Circular LOOK. Combines C-SCAN's uniformity with LOOK's efficiency. Best practical algorithm. Returns to first request (not to cylinder 0) after reaching the last request. n Solved Numerical — C-SCAN Head = 50, moving RIGHT, Requests: 82, 170, 43, 140, 24, 16, 190 (max cylinder = 199) C-SCAN sequence: 50 → 82 → 140 → 170 → 190 → (jump to 0) → 16 → 24 → 43 Distances: 32+58+30+20+9 + jump + 16+8+19 = 149 + jump(191) + 43 = 383 cylinders Compare SCAN: 50→82→140→170→190→43→24→16 = 140+147 = 287 cylinders SCAN has less movement here; C-SCAN trades movement for more uniform wait times. 5.3 I/O Systems

[Page 19] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 19 Polling (Busy-Wait) CPU repeatedly checks a status register of the device in a tight loop: 'Ready? Ready? Ready?'. Simple to implement. Wastes CPU cycles. OK only if device responds very quickly. Example: some embedded systems. Interrupts Device signals CPU via an interrupt request (IRQ) when ready. CPU can do other work between I/O initiation and completion. Interrupt handler (ISR) processes the event. More efficient than polling for most devices. DMA (Direct Memory Access) A DMA controller transfers data directly between device and RAM without CPU involvement for each byte. CPU only initiates transfer and is interrupted when complete. Fastest I/O method. Used by disk drives, network cards, graphics cards. Buffering Store data in a buffer while transferring to/from device. Handles speed mismatch between producer and consumer. Single buffer, double buffer, circular buffer schemes.

[Page 20] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 20 6. Linux / Unix & Specialised Topics 6.1 Linux File System Hierarchy & Permissions Linux/Unix — File Permissions (chmod) & Process Hierarchy File Permission Structure - Type rwx Owner r-x Group r-- Others iv: Owner=7(rwx) Group=5(r-x) Others=4(r--) chmod 754 file.txt r=4 Read file / list directory w=2 Write / modify / delete x=1 Execute file / enter directory 0 No permission Linux Process Hierarchy init/systemd (PID=1) bash (shell) ls -la grep pattern python script fork() creates child processes / — Root Top of the entire file system tree. Everything is under this. /bin Essential user binaries: ls, cp, mv, cat, grep, chmod /sbin System admin binaries: fdisk, ifconfig, init, shutdown /etc System configuration files: passwd, fstab, crontab, network settings /home User home directories: /home/alice, /home/bob /var Variable data that changes: logs (/var/log), mail, databases /tmp Temporary files — cleared on reboot /proc Virtual filesystem — live kernel and process info (not on disk) /dev Device files: /dev/sda (disk), /dev/tty (terminal) /usr User programs: /usr/bin, /usr/lib, /usr/share 6.2 Essential Linux Commands Command Purpose & Notes ls -la List ALL files (including hidden dotfiles), with permissions, owner, size, date

[Page 21] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 21 chmod 755 file Change permissions: 7=rwx(owner), 5=r-x(group), 5=r-x(others) chown user:grp f Change ownership of file to user:group grep -rn 'pat' . Search recursively for pattern, show line numbers awk '{print $2}' Print second column of each line — powerful text processing sed 's/old/new/g' Replace all occurrences of 'old' with 'new' in file output ps aux Show ALL running processes with PID, CPU%, MEM%, command kill -9 PID Forcibly terminate process (SIGKILL — cannot be caught/ignored) top / htop Real-time process monitor — CPU, memory usage live df -h Disk free space for all mounted filesystems (human-readable) free -m RAM and swap usage in megabytes find / -name f Recursively find file named 'f' starting from root tar -czf a.tgz d Create compressed archive of directory d ssh user@host Secure shell — remote login to another machine netstat -tuln Show listening TCP/UDP ports and their status 6.3 Windows vs Linux — Key Differences Kernel Type Linux: Monolithic with loadable modules (drivers loaded/unloaded at runtime). Windows: Hybrid kernel (NT kernel — performance-critical parts in kernel, services as user-mode processes). Process Scheduling Linux: CFS — Completely Fair Scheduler. Uses 'virtual runtime' to ensure all processes get equal CPU share proportional to their weight/priority. Windows: Preemptive multilevel priority-based scheduling with time-slicing. File System Linux: ext4 (default), also XFS, Btrfs, ZFS, tmpfs. Windows: NTFS (default), also FAT32, exFAT. Linux mounts everything under / — Windows uses drive letters (C:, D:). Security Model Linux: DAC — Discretionary Access Control (rwx permissions for owner/group/others) + optional SELinux/AppArmor for MAC. Windows: ACL — Access Control Lists (more granular, per-user/group permissions). Shell & CLI Linux: bash (default), also zsh, fish, sh. Shell scripting is first-class. Windows: PowerShell, CMD. PowerShell also powerful but traditionally less used for system automation. Open Source Linux: fully open source (GPL) — anyone can read/modify kernel source. Windows: proprietary — source code not publicly available. 6.4 Distributed OS Concepts

[Page 22] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 22 A Distributed OS manages a collection of independent computers and makes them appear as a single coherent system to users. Transparency User is unaware of the underlying distribution. Types: access transparency (same operations for local/remote), location transparency (don't know where resource is), replication transparency, failure transparency. Scalability System can grow in size (more users), geography (more locations), and administration without loss of performance. Horizontal scaling: add more machines. Vertical: upgrade machines. RPC (Remote Procedure Call) Client calls a function that actually executes on a remote server — looks like a local call to the programmer. Stub functions handle marshalling (packing) parameters and network transmission. Example: gRPC, XML-RPC, Java RMI. Fault Tolerance System continues operating despite partial failures. Achieved through replication (keep multiple copies), checkpointing (save state periodically), and redundancy.

[Page 23] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 23 7. Formula Sheet & Quick Reference Scheduling Formulas Completion Time (CT) = Time at which process finishes execution Turnaround Time (TAT) = CT − Arrival Time (total time in system) Waiting Time (WT) = TAT − Burst Time (time spent waiting in ready queue) Response Time (RT) = Time of first CPU allocation − Arrival Time Avg Waiting Time = Σ(WT for all processes) / Number of processes Avg Turnaround Time = Σ(TAT for all processes) / Number of processes CPU Utilisation % = (Total Burst Time) / (Total Elapsed Time) × 100 Throughput = Number of processes completed per unit time Memory Management Formulas Page Number (p) = n Logical Address / Page Size n (integer division) Offset (d) = Logical Address mod Page Size Physical Address = Frame Number × Page Size + Offset Number of Pages = Logical Address Space / Page Size Number of Frames = Physical Memory Size / Page Size Page Table Size = Number of Pages × Size of each entry EAT (with TLB) = α × t_TLB + (1 − α) × (t_TLB + t_mem) where α = hit ratio EAT (2-level page table) = t_TLB + 2 × t_mem (on TLB miss) Internal Fragmentation = Frame Size − (Process Size mod Frame Size) Disk Scheduling Formulas Total Seek Distance = Sum of |cylinder_i+1 − cylinder_i| for each step in service sequence Average Seek Time = Total Seek Distance / Number of Requests Disk Access Time = Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time Rotational Latency (avg) = 0.5 × (1 / RPM × 60) seconds = half a rotation on average Transfer Rate = Disk Speed (bytes/sec) = Bytes per track × Rotations per second

[Page 24] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 24 Key Comparisons to Memorise Topic Option A Option B Winner / Note Speed Monolithic Kernel Microkernel Monolithic (no msg passing) Safety Microkernel Monolithic Microkernel (isolated crashes) Page Replacement Optimal LRU Optimal (theoretical best) Belady's Anomaly FIFO LRU / Optimal FIFO suffers; LRU/OPT don't IPC Speed Shared Memory Message Passing Shared Memory (no kernel per op) Disk Uniformity C-SCAN / C-LOOK SCAN C-SCAN more uniform wait Disk Throughput SSTF FCFS SSTF (but can starve) CPU Avg WT SJF/SRTF FCFS SJF optimal avg wait time Deadlock Avoidance (Banker's) Prevention Avoidance allows more resource use

[Page 25] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 25 8. Practice Numericals — Fully Solved Problem 1 — SJF Scheduling (Non-Preemptive) n Given Processes: P1(AT=0,BT=7), P2(AT=2,BT=4), P3(AT=4,BT=1), P4(AT=5,BT=4) 3 Solution At t=0: only P1 ready → run P1. P1 finishes at t=7. At t=7: P2,P3,P4 all arrived → shortest burst = P3(BT=1) → run P3 (t=7-8) At t=8: P2(BT=4), P4(BT=4) tied → pick P2 → run P2 (t=8-12) At t=12: P4 → run P4 (t=12-16) Gantt: |P1(0-7)|P3(7-8)|P2(8-12)|P4(12-16)| Completion: P1=7, P2=12, P3=8, P4=16 TAT: P1=7, P2=10, P3=4, P4=11 Avg TAT = 32/4 = 8.0 ms WT: P1=0, P2=6, P3=3, P4=7 Avg WT = 16/4 = 4.0 ms Problem 2 — FIFO Page Replacement (Belady's Anomaly Demo) n Given Reference string: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 Test with 3 frames AND 4 frames to observe Belady's Anomaly 3 Solution 3 Frames: 1(F),2(F),3(F),4(F→evict1),1(F→evict2),2(F→evict3),5(F→evict4), 1(F→evict5),2(F),3(F),4(F),5(F) → Total FAULTS = 9 4 Frames: 1(F),2(F),3(F),4(F),1(H),2(H),5(F→evict1),1(F),2(H),3(H),4(F),5(H) → Total FAULTS = 10 More frames (4) caused MORE faults (10) than fewer frames (3 → 9 faults)! This is Belady's Anomaly — only happens with FIFO, NOT with LRU or Optimal. Problem 3 — SCAN Disk Scheduling n Given Head = 100, moving toward higher cylinders, Disk = 0-199 Requests: 55, 58, 39, 18, 90, 160, 150, 38, 184 3 Solution Sort requests: 18, 38, 39, 55, 58, 90, 150, 160, 184 Moving right from 100: service 150, 160, 184 → reach end (199) → reverse Moving left: service 90, 58, 55, 39, 38, 18 Sequence: 100→150→160→184→(199)→90→58→55→39→38→18 Movement: 50+10+24+15 (right part) + 109+32+3+16+1+20 (left part) = 280 cylinders For LOOK: Stop at 184 (last request rightward), no trip to 199 LOOK movement = 50+10+24 + 94+32+3+16+1+20 = 250 cylinders (saves 30 vs SCAN)

[Page 26] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 26 Problem 4 — Banker's Algorithm Safe State n Given 5 Processes (P0-P4), 3 Resource Types A,B,C Allocation: P0=[0,1,0] P1=[2,0,0] P2=[3,0,2] P3=[2,1,1] P4=[0,0,2] Max: P0=[7,5,3] P1=[3,2,2] P2=[9,0,2] P3=[2,2,2] P4=[4,3,3] Available = [3,3,2] 3 Solution Step 1: Need = Max - Allocation P0=[7,4,3] P1=[1,2,2] P2=[6,0,0] P3=[0,1,1] P4=[4,3,1] Step 2: Find process where Need ≤ Available [3,3,2] P1 need=[1,2,2] ≤ [3,3,2] 3 → Run P1, Available=[3,3,2]+[2,0,0]=[5,3,2] P3 need=[0,1,1] ≤ [5,3,2] 3 → Run P3, Available=[5,3,2]+[2,1,1]=[7,4,3] P4 need=[4,3,1] ≤ [7,4,3] 3 → Run P4, Available=[7,4,3]+[0,0,2]=[7,4,5] P0 need=[7,4,3] ≤ [7,4,5] 3 → Run P0, Available=[7,5,5] P2 need=[6,0,0] ≤ [7,5,5] 3 → Run P2 Safe Sequence: P1 → P3 → P4 → P0 → P2 3 SYSTEM IS IN SAFE STATE

[Page 27] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 27 9. UPPSC Exam Strategy Topic Weightage Guide Topic Expected Questions Difficulty Key Focus Disk Scheduling Numericals 3–5 Medium SCAN,C-SCAN,SSTF movement calculation Page Replacement Numericals 3–5 Medium LRU, FIFO trace; count faults; Belady's CPU Scheduling Numericals 2–4 Medium Gantt charts, WT, TAT calculations Deadlock Theory 2–3 Easy-Med 4 conditions, Banker's safe state Semaphores / Synchronisation 1–3 Medium wait/signal logic, classic problems Kernel Types 1–2 Easy Monolithic vs Microkernel tradeoffs Linux Commands 1–3 Easy chmod, grep, ps, kill, find Memory: Paging/TLB 1–2 Med-Hard Address translation, EAT formula Process States 1–2 Easy State transitions, what triggers each Distributed OS / IPC 1–2 Easy RPC, transparency, shared mem vs msg Master Numericals First Numericals carry the most marks. Practice disk scheduling (SCAN, C-SCAN, SSTF) and page replacement (LRU trace, FIFO) until you can solve them under 60 seconds. Timing yourself is essential. Theory: Know the WHY UPPSC asks 'why' questions, not just definitions. Example: 'Why does Microkernel sacrifice speed for safety?' Know the mechanism, not just the label. Every key concept has a cause-and-effect explanation. The Linux Factor Unlike GATE (which focuses on pure theory), UPPSC Lecturer posts test Linux/Unix basics. Know chmod notation, read/write/execute values (r=4, w=2, x=1), common commands, and the filesystem hierarchy /etc /var /proc. Deadlock — 4 Conditions All four conditions must hold simultaneously for deadlock. Know each one. Critical trap: Banker's Algorithm is AVOIDANCE (not prevention). Prevention eliminates a condition; avoidance checks safe state before each allocation.

[Page 28] OS Complete Visual Study Guide — UPPSC Lecturer Exam | Page 28 Semaphore Values Memorise: S>0 means that many resources free. S=0 means no resource, nobody waiting. S<0 means |S| processes are blocked in the wait queue. This logic appears directly in exam questions. Draw State Diagrams from Memory For process states and scheduling, drawing the diagram helps in the exam. Practice drawing the 5-state model with all transitions labelled. Same for the paging address translation path and semaphore flow. All the best for your UPPSC Lecturer Examination! Master the diagrams, practise the numericals, and understand the WHY behind every concept.
